metadata.title: Revision Change Log: Make Drift Visible
metadata.description_blurb: 'Make it better' tells Claude to make it different — and without a change log, you cannot tell what moved, so the next revision is also blind.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: She sent three revision prompts: make it more engaging, tighten the structure, sharpen the conclusion. Three versions came back. The third was polished. It was also missing the three statistics from the second paragraph and had dropped the cautious register of the original. She had not asked to remove them. She had not noticed they were gone. She had not made it better. She had made it different — and she could not trace what had moved. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, vague revision prompt

props.command: Make it more engaging and fix the structure.

beats.narration_text: A vague revision prompt — make it better, tighten it, improve the flow — tells Claude to make it different. Claude has no way to know what you valued in the original, what must be preserved, or which change counts as improvement versus drift. The output changes. Whether it improves depends entirely on a comparison you have to make. Without a change log, that comparison is invisible.
beats.new_visual_element: Different not better — vague revision / no preservation constraint / change without comparison = drift

beats.narration_text: The revision loop has four steps: name the failure category, state the preservation constraint, request the change, require a change log. Not 'make it better' but 'the opening paragraph buries the claim.' Not open-ended but 'keep all statistics exactly as written.' The change log converts revision from replacement to accountable editing: what moved, what changed in language, what was kept exactly.
beats.new_visual_element: Diagnose first — name failure / preservation constraint / request change / change log required
beats.spark_line: Name the failure. Name the wall.

beats.narration_text: The change log request is three lines appended to the revision prompt. After revising, list: what you moved or restructured, what language you changed, what you kept exactly. The log catches implicit overwrites — changes to content you did not ask to change — before they compound across three more revisions. When the log shows a statistic removed you did not flag, you catch it before the next draft.
beats.new_visual_element: Change log format — moved/restructured / language changed / kept exactly / catches implicit overwrites

beats.narration_text: A revision prompt is a second specification. It needs a failure description, a preservation wall, and a verification method — the change log. Without the log, revision accumulates drift and the next prompt is sent against a version you cannot account for. With the log, each revision is auditable. You approve what moved, reject what should not have, and the next revision builds on something real.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — revision prompt + change log / drift caught / next revision builds on accountable version

beats.narration_text: Add the change log request to your next revision prompt. Paste this.
beats.new_visual_element: HANDOFF — revision prompt with change log

props.command: Revise this for the following reason: [name the specific failure — e.g., the opening buries the claim]. Preservation constraint: [what must not change — e.g., keep all statistics exactly as written, keep the cautious register]. After revising, provide a change log: (1) what you moved or restructured, (2) what language you changed and why, (3) what you kept exactly as written. [paste text]

beats.narration_text: If this makes the next revision build on something you can account for, subscribe. Liam, in for Bear.
